_note: Anna article_drill batch 1 — 15 items at B1, distributed across the 5-pattern taxonomy from article_diagnostic_2026-04-05.html: a_the_swap (5), phantom_article (4), prep_article (2), fixed_expression (2), dropped_article (2). Themes: home, family, padel, neighbours, daily routine, kids. Single-blank scenes; player taps a/an/the/— chip. Reasoning shown after every answer (correct or wrong). Patterns are recorded in matched_pattern_id for stats aggregation.

exercises.sentence_template: We bought a new sofa last week. {1} sofa is really comfortable.

blanks.reasoning: Second mention. Once a noun is introduced with 'a', switch to 'the' on every later mention — both speaker and listener now know which sofa. Russian 'диван' gives no marker, so this swap is the highest-frequency error.

exercises.fallback_feedback: Second-mention rule: introduce with 'a', refer back with 'the'.

exercises.sentence_template: I went to the padel club yesterday. {1} courts were really busy.

blanks.reasoning: Situational unique — once you mention 'the padel club', both of us know which courts (the ones at that club). Heuristic: 'Do we both know which one?' → yes → THE.

exercises.fallback_feedback: Once a place is established, things at that place take 'the' (the courts, the entrance, the bar).

exercises.sentence_template: Tell {1} kids that dinner is ready.

blanks.reasoning: Your specific kids — both speaker and listener know which kids. 'The' marks definite reference. Russian 'детям' gives no article cue, so it's easy to drop or substitute.

exercises.fallback_feedback: When the noun refers to specific known people, use 'the'.

exercises.sentence_template: Did you talk to {1} neighbours about the noise last night?

blanks.reasoning: Shared knowledge — 'the neighbours' means our specific neighbours, not just any neighbours. Heuristic: 'Do we both know which ones?' → yes → THE.

exercises.fallback_feedback: Specific people we both know about take 'the'.

exercises.sentence_template: I made a smoothie this morning. {1} smoothie was really refreshing.

blanks.reasoning: Second mention. Introduced with 'a' (any smoothie), referred back with 'the' (now we both know which one — the one I just made).

exercises.fallback_feedback: a → the on every reference back to the same noun.

exercises.sentence_template: Can you give me {1} advice about cooking lamb?

blanks.reasoning: 'Advice' is uncountable in English — no 'a/an', no plural. Russian 'совет' is countable, so 'give me an advice' is a direct calque. ✓ 'give me advice' / 'give me some advice' / 'give me a piece of advice'.

exercises.fallback_feedback: Uncountable nouns (advice, information, progress, news) take no 'a/an'.

exercises.sentence_template: We're going to the beach {1} next Saturday.

blanks.reasoning: next/last + day or time = no article. ✗ 'the next Saturday' / 'a next Saturday'. ✓ 'next Saturday' / 'last Monday' / 'next week'.

exercises.fallback_feedback: Time expressions with next/last take zero article.

exercises.sentence_template: {1} life in Bahrain is calm and easy.

blanks.reasoning: Abstract noun in general sense — 'life' here means life-in-general, no article. Russian 'жизнь' sits naturally as a subject, which makes the urge to add 'the' feel right, but it's wrong for the general meaning.

exercises.fallback_feedback: Abstract nouns used generically (life, love, happiness, music) take no article.

exercises.sentence_template: We always have a barbecue on {1} Friday evenings.

blanks.reasoning: Days of the week in plural + habitual events = no article. ✗ 'on the Friday evenings' / 'on a Friday evening' (for a habit). ✓ 'on Fridays' / 'on Friday evenings'.

exercises.fallback_feedback: Habitual time expressions (on Mondays, in the evenings) take no article on the day.

exercises.sentence_template: Our new neighbours moved in {1} March.

blanks.reasoning: Months take no article after 'in'. ✗ 'in the March' / 'in a March'. ✓ 'in March' / 'in April' / 'in summer'.

exercises.fallback_feedback: in + month / season / year = no article on the time word.

exercises.sentence_template: I really like the smell of {1} coffee in the morning.

blanks.reasoning: 'Coffee' here is uncountable/general — the smell of coffee-in-general, not any specific coffee. Pattern: 'the [N] of [uncountable Y]' keeps zero article on Y. ✓ 'the taste of milk', 'the sound of music'.

exercises.fallback_feedback: When the second noun is uncountable and general, it stays bare.

exercises.sentence_template: Sasha is making {1} good progress at school.

blanks.reasoning: 'Make progress' is a fixed phrase — no article, even with an adjective in front. ✗ 'make a good progress' (calque from Russian 'хороший прогресс'). ✓ 'make good progress' / 'make great progress' / 'make slow progress'.

exercises.fallback_feedback: Fixed phrases (make progress, take action, give advice) carry zero article.

exercises.sentence_template: Sasha goes to {1} school by bus every morning.

blanks.reasoning: 'Go to school' (as a student, the institution sense) takes zero article. Same with 'go to work' / 'go to bed' / 'go to church'. ⚠ 'go to the school' would mean visiting the building (e.g. to pick someone up).

exercises.fallback_feedback: Institutions in role sense (school, work, hospital, prison) take zero article.

blanks.reasoning: Professions take 'a/an' — this is 'classifying A' (saying what kind of person). ✗ 'is engineer' (direct calque from Russian 'Он инженер'). ✓ 'is an engineer' / 'is a teacher' / 'is a doctor'. (Vowel sound → 'an'.)

exercises.fallback_feedback: She/he is + a/an + profession. Russian drops the article — English requires it.

exercises.sentence_template: There is {1} new café on our street.

blanks.reasoning: First mention of a singular countable noun — needs 'a'. ✗ 'there is new café' (Russian 'есть новое кафе' has no article). ✓ 'there is a new café'. Heuristic: 'Do we both know which one?' → no, it's new info → A.

exercises.fallback_feedback: Introducing a new singular countable noun requires 'a' (or 'an' before vowel sounds).
